explicit_text: My name is Lily Oki, and I was a third grader when the war started. I remember a classmate recognizing my name years later and being surprised because I disappeared from school during the war. My family was taken to the Pomona Assembly Center, and life changed drastically after that. I grew up in Los Angeles, and my experiences during and after the war shaped much of my childhood.

explicit_text: The war began with the December 7 attack, which changed everything for my family and me. I remember we had our Christmas tree up, and my father took a last family picture before turning in his cameras and swords to the police. That day marked the start of a difficult time, leading to our forced removal and eventual internment.

explicit_text: My family and I were sent to the Pomona Assembly Center after the war started. It was set up in a parking lot with eucalyptus trees and fenced in to become a temporary camp. We only had a small room there, and my mother brought her sewing machine, which helped us make curtains and clothes. It was a difficult place, but we knew Pomona from their fairgrounds before the war.

explicit_text: We took the bus from near MacArthur Park, which was called that even then, to the assembly center. My mother, brother, and I went down to the bus near Alvarado and Sixth Street, now MacArthur Park. My father drove his work truck there and brought in my mother's sewing machine and some goods before we left.

explicit_text: My mother was born in Long Beach, California, though her family returned to Japan when she was young. explicit_text: Setsuo was my mother's brother who lived with us for a time, and I always looked up to him like he was Superman. He was strong, did judo, and was tall and husky. After the war started, he took the last ship back to Japan because his mother was ill, and he was drafted into the Japanese army and sent to Burma.

explicit_text: My uncle Setsuo was sent to fight in the Burma Campaign during the war after being drafted into the Japanese army.
